title: dsherret/ts-morph — TypeScript AST Wrapper for Static Analysis
insights: ts-morph: TypeScript Compiler API wrapper. Foundation for building custom design system scanners: find all JSX elements with a given import source, detect prop usage patterns, identify deprecated API usage, generate migration codemods. Not a ready-made tool — a library for building tools. Used to build custom adoption scanners and codemod runners in TypeScript.

title: ts-morph — Navigating the AST
insights: API: getSourceFiles(), getClasses(), getInterfaces(), getJsxElements(). For design system scanning: traverse all JSX opening elements, filter by import source (e.g., @myorg/design-system), collect prop names and values. ts-morph adds TypeScript type resolution on top of raw AST — identifies the actual type of a prop value, not just the string.

title: Luro — Build and Track Component Usage Across Products
insights: Luro: design system management platform focused on component lifecycle and adoption. Features: component registry with ownership, usage tracking across repos, adoption metrics, deprecation workflows (mark deprecated, set sunset date, notify consumers). Alternative to Omlet with stronger governance workflow (ownership, sunset dates) and weaker analytics depth.

title: Design Systems and AI: Why MCP Servers Are The Unlock — Figma Blog
insights: Figma's thesis: design system governance shifts when AI agents have design context. The MCP server delivers Figma variables, components, tokens, and Code Connect snippets to AI coding agents. When AI builds with design system context: it reuses existing components, applies tokens automatically, and shortens feedback loops. Governance becomes the infrastructure AI trains on.

title: Introducing Figma Dev Mode MCP Server — Figma Blog
insights: Figma MCP server: GA (out of beta 2025). Brings design context into Cursor, Copilot, Claude Code, Windsurf. When a frame is inspected: sends components, styles, variables, and Code Connect snippets to the AI agent. Remote access mode available (browser-based agents can now call the MCP). Governance implication: if your design system docs are in Figma + Code Connect, AI agents use them correctly.

title: Figma MCP + Code Connect Integration — Developer Docs
insights: Code Connect: maps Figma components to codebase implementations. When MCP server processes a frame with Code Connect components, it injects real import paths, prop types, and usage examples into the AI context. Two setup paths: Code Connect UI (no-code, Figma-side) and Code Connect CLI (code-side, for complex components). Critical for AI-accurate code generation.

title: Code Connect — Figma Help Center
insights: Code Connect bridges Figma Dev Mode and your codebase. Setup: install @figma/code-connect CLI, run figma connect create to generate stub files, fill in actual import paths and prop mappings, run figma connect publish. Once published: designers see real component code in Dev Mode inspect, AI agents get correct implementation context via MCP.

title: figma/sds — Simple Design System Reference Implementation
insights: Figma's official reference design system: shows how Figma Variables, Styles, Components, and Code Connect work alongside a React codebase. Open source on GitHub. Demonstrates: token file structure (W3C DTCG format), Code Connect setup, Storybook integration. The canonical example Figma publishes for their governance stack.

title: W3C DTCG — Design Tokens Specification Reaches First Stable Version
insights: W3C Design Tokens Community Group published DTCG spec v1.0 (2025.10) on October 28, 2025. First stable, vendor-neutral format for sharing design decisions. JSON-based: application/design-tokens+json, file extensions .tokens or .tokens.json. Supports: composite tokens, theming, modern color spaces (oklch, p3). 10+ tools implementing: Figma, Penpot, Sketch, Framer, Tokens Studio, Style Dictionary, Supernova, Zeroheight. Not a W3C Standard (Community Group report).

title: Design Tokens Format Module 2025.10 — Official Spec
insights: DTCG 2025.10 spec: $value, $type, $description, $extensions fields. Token types: color, dimension, fontFamily, fontWeight, number, duration, cubicBezier, shadow, strokeStyle, border, typography, transition, composite. Theming via token references ($value can reference another token with curly-brace syntax). Breaking change from earlier DTCG drafts: $ prefix added to reserved keys.

title: Style Dictionary — DTCG Format Support
insights: Style Dictionary v4 has first-class DTCG format support. v5 (in progress) adds full 2025.10 format support. API: transforms, transformGroups, formats — convert tokens to CSS custom properties, SCSS variables, iOS Swift, Android XML, JSON. The de facto transform layer between the source-of-truth tokens file and platform-specific output. Used by most mature design systems.

title: Design Tokens in Practice: From Figma Variables to Production Code
insights: Full pipeline walkthrough: Figma Variables → Tokens Studio sync to GitHub → Style Dictionary v4 transform → CI publishes CSS/JS tokens to npm. Decision point: Figma Variables vs Tokens Studio for token creation. Figma Variables native for simpler systems, Tokens Studio for complex multi-theme/multi-platform. W3C spec alignment critical for tool interoperability.

title: From Figma to Production: How I Finally Synced My Design System
insights: Practical guide: Tokens Studio GitHub sync → on PR, GitHub Action runs Style Dictionary → generates CSS variables → publishes to npm. Key insight: Figma is the design source but Git is the code source. Two-way sync breaks down — treat Figma as input, Git as canonical. Drift occurs when designers update Figma without syncing (manual export anti-pattern).

title: Design System Mastery with Figma Variables 2025-2026 Best-Practice Playbook
insights: 2025 best practices: 3-4 Figma variable collections (primitives, semantic, component, brand). Modes: light/dark, density (compact/default), brand (for white-label). Name variables to match CSS custom property names directly. Use Figma Variables REST API for programmatic export (no plugin required in 2025). Theming via modes = one token file, multiple outputs.

title: Schema 2025: Design Systems For A New Era — Figma Blog
insights: Figma Schema 2025 conference recap: AI-native design workflow emerging. Code Connect GA. Figma MCP server GA. Variables REST API improvements. Design systems becoming the training data for AI agents. The governance question shifts: who owns the MCP server configuration? Who writes Code Connect mappings? New role emerging: design system AI liaison.

title: Nextra — Next.js Static Site Generator for Docs
insights: Nextra v4: opinionated Next.js doc framework. MDX with React components inline. Full Next.js feature set (SSR, ISR, API routes). Default themes: Docs (sidebar + TOC) and Blog. Ships 200-400KB JavaScript — heaviest of the three SSGs. Best for teams already in Next.js ecosystem wanting to embed React components (interactive demos, live prop editors) in docs. Vercel-hosted first-class support.

title: shuding/nextra — GitHub Repository
insights: Nextra GitHub: active development. v4 released 2024. Used by: Next.js docs, SWR docs, Turbo docs, thousands of open-source projects. Plugin API for custom theme layers. Built-in: full-text search, dark mode, i18n, syntax highlighting, MDX. Design systems using Nextra: typically those already deployed on Vercel.

title: Astro Starlight — Documentation Sites Built with Astro
insights: Starlight: Astro-based SSG. Ships <50KB JavaScript (island architecture — static HTML by default). Build: 1000-page site in ~18s vs ~52s for Nextra. Built-in: sidebar from file structure, i18n with language switcher, Zod frontmatter validation, OpenGraph image generation, Tabs/Cards/Asides components, built-in search. Tailwind integration one-line. Multi-framework: React, Vue, Svelte, Solid components.

title: Fumadocs vs Nextra v4 vs Starlight 2026 — PkgPulse
insights: Three-way comparison 2026: Fumadocs (headless Next.js kit — maximum customization, no opinionated theme), Nextra v4 (opinionated Next.js theme, fastest for React teams), Starlight (best out-of-box features, smallest JS, best SEO). For design system docs: Starlight wins on performance and built-in features; Nextra wins for React-heavy interactive demos; Fumadocs wins for white-label custom portals.

title: Docusaurus — Open Source Documentation Site Framework
insights: Docusaurus: Meta (Facebook) maintained. Most mature (since 2017). Versioned docs support (critical for multi-version design systems). Plugin system. MDX. i18n. Infima CSS framework tightly coupled. More opinionated than Starlight. Best feature for design systems: built-in versioned documentation (docs for v2, v3, v4 simultaneously), which Starlight lacks natively as of 2025.

title: Backstage Design System — Official Docs
insights: Backstage UI (BUI): new design system replacing Material UI. Storybook as component reference. Figma UI Kit for plugin designers. Backstage positions as the platform for registering and cataloging design systems alongside other software components. New: Actions Registry (v1.40) with MCP tool integration. Not primarily a design system tool — it's a developer portal that can host design system component catalogs.

title: backstage/backstage — GitHub Repository (30K+ stars)
insights: Backstage: 30K+ stars. CNCF project. Used at Spotify, Netflix, Airbnb, American Airlines. Software catalog model applies to design systems: every component is a 'Component' entity in the catalog with owner, tier, status. Techdocs integration for automatically publishing markdown docs. Design system team can catalog all their components with health scores, adoption rates, ownership.

title: Carbon Design System RFCs — GitHub Repository
insights: IBM Carbon's formal RFC process: proposals submitted as PRs to rfcs repo. RFC template: motivation, proposal, drawbacks, alternatives, unresolved questions. Final Comment Period (FCP): 3 calendar days after maintainer motion. RFC merged = approved for implementation. Community can comment throughout. Active from 2019-2024. Pattern: large open-source design systems need formal RFC gates to prevent ad-hoc breaking changes.

title: Australian Government Design System RFCs
insights: Gov AU design system RFC process: three stages — draft (PR discussion), approved (merged to 1-approved/), ready-for-implementation. Absolute majority of maintainers required for approval. Open source, government design system showing RFC governance at scale across multiple agencies.

title: Atlassian Design System Contribution Overview
insights: Atlassian contribution tiers: Core (reviewed by DS team, assigned point of contact), Extended (product team owns), Out of Scope. Process: file issue → classification → design review → code review → accessibility review → docs review → release. Figma + code must both be contributed together. Contribution classified as core requires Atlassian DS team approval. Governance at Atlassian: 'local design systems' model where product teams inherit foundations and extend with their own layer.

title: GitLab Pajamas Design System Contribution Guide
insights: Pajamas contribution: issue/MR opens conversation → core/extended/out-of-scope classification → assigned DS team point of contact for core → collaborative design+code phase → review against 5 criteria (accessibility, visual consistency, documentation completeness, test coverage, adoption potential). GitLab design system is a benchmark for open-source governance with visible process documentation.

title: GitHub Primer Design System Contribution Guidelines
insights: Primer's contribution guidelines (updated Oct 2025): new component proposals require issue with use cases, existing component coverage analysis, cross-team impact assessment. RFC-style discussion in GitHub Issues before implementation. Design and code contributions must be paired. Primer is GitHub's own design system — highest visibility internal governance example.

title: GOV.UK Design System Contribution Criteria
insights: GOV.UK DS criteria for contribution acceptance: component must be useful (evidence of need in ≥3 services), unique (not duplicate of existing), good (meets WCAG 2.2, tested with users, research-backed). Governance is research-gated — no component without user research evidence. Sets the gold standard for evidence-based contribution governance in public sector.

title: Shopify Polaris Design System
insights: Polaris: Shopify's design system. Governance model: open source with Shopify team maintaining core. New component proposals require issue with template (problem, proposed solution, cross-team review). Polaris ships codemods with every major version — developers run one command to auto-migrate breaking changes across their codebase. Tokens managed in polaris-tokens package, synced to Figma.

title: Shopify Polaris CONTRIBUTING.md
insights: Polaris contribution process: file issue before significant work → design proposal (for new components or token changes) → code patch → PR review (product + design + platform engineering). Template-driven: contribution type templates lower friction while ensuring consistency. SemVer with codemods per major.

title: Automating Design System Evolution with Codemods — Hypermod
insights: Hypermod platform for design system codemod automation: AI agents plan the migration, deterministic codemods execute. Features: scan codebase for deprecated API usage, estimate migration scope, auto-generate codemod from old → new API, run across multiple repos. 50-80% efficiency gain on large migrations. Design system teams ship codemods alongside deprecation notices. Open beta Jan 2025.

title: Hypermod — Automate Dependency Upgrades and Migrations
insights: Hypermod: codemod-as-a-service. Connect repo, define migration (old import → new import + prop rename), Hypermod generates and runs the codemod across all files. Community codemod library. Free for public/OSS repos. Case: migrated 4,000 token references in minutes vs weeks manual. For design systems: ships codemods as first-class artifacts with every breaking change.

title: Automate Design Token Migrations with Codemods
insights: Token migration codemod case study: organization migrated from old semantic token names to new DTCG-aligned names. 2,500 files, 4,000 token references. Codemod written with jscodeshift: find all CSS variable usages matching old-token pattern, replace with new-token. Took 5 days to write, ran in minutes. 80% automated, 20% manual edge cases. Critical insight: document the 20% in migration guide.

title: Refactoring with Codemods to Automate API Changes — Martin Fowler Blog
insights: Martin Fowler on codemods: treat them as first-class deliverables alongside API changes. Three types: mechanical (pure rename), structural (prop consolidation), behavioral (logic change requiring human review). Design system pattern: ship codemod in same PR as breaking change. npx @myorg/ds-codemods v5-migration runs all mechanical transforms. Behavioral changes need human-reviewed migration guide.

title: Versioning Design Systems — Nathan Curtis, EightShapes
insights: Nathan Curtis: the definitive reference on design system versioning strategy. Key insight: version the system, not just the code. Design files, tokens, documentation must version in sync with the code package. Breaking change in design (token rename) = major version bump even if the CSS output is unchanged. Communication calendar: announce deprecation → warn → remove across 2+ major versions.

title: USWDS Component Lifecycle — U.S. Web Design System
insights: USWDS component lifecycle: Proposal → Experimental → Candidate → Released → Deprecated → Retired. Each stage has criteria. Experimental = usable but API unstable. Candidate = API stable, gathering feedback. Released = production-ready, SemVer applies. Deprecated = will be removed (timeline published). The most formal public sector lifecycle model — widely referenced by other government and enterprise design systems.

title: Team Models for Scaling a Design System — Nathan Curtis, EightShapes
insights: Four team models: Solitary (one person), Centralized (dedicated team), Federated (representatives from product teams), Hybrid (dedicated core + federated contributors). For scaling: Hybrid model dominates at >200 engineers. Centralized fails because it becomes a bottleneck. Federated fails without strong governance guardrails. Hybrid: core team owns quality gates, federated contributors own the contribution pipeline.

title: Design Systems Report 2025 Full Report — Zeroheight
insights: Full 2025 report findings: token adoption 84% (was ~60% 2023). 79% have dedicated team. Top challenges: resource shortage, communication, cross-platform consistency. Best predictor of adoption success: having a community-building role. Measurement gap: teams track component coverage but not velocity impact. 2025 focus areas: AI tooling integration, token pipeline automation.

title: Turborepo Design System Template — Vercel
insights: Official Turborepo design system starter: monorepo with packages/ui (components), packages/tokens, apps/docs (Storybook), apps/web (consumer). Changesets for versioning. Turbo pipeline: lint → build → test → storybook. Remote caching reduces CI time. Recommended starting structure for new design systems.

title: Turborepo Examples: Design System — GitHub
insights: Vercel's official design system monorepo example. Structure: packages/ui, packages/eslint-config, packages/typescript-config, apps/docs, apps/web. Turborepo pipeline: build depends on ^build (topological). Changesets integrated. Each package has independent package.json with publishConfig. Pattern: this is the reference architecture most teams copy for new design system monorepos in 2025.

title: Versioning and Releasing Packages in a Monorepo — Nx Blog
insights: Nx native release (v17+): nx release version (determines SemVer from conventional commits), nx release changelog (generates CHANGELOG), nx release publish (publishes to npm). Replaces Changesets for NX-managed monorepos. Configurable: independent vs synchronized versioning modes. Changelog granularity: per-project or aggregated. NX is increasingly competing with Changesets for monorepo design systems.

title: The New Business Case for Design Systems — Figma Blog
insights: Updated ROI framework: design system ROI now includes AI efficiency multiplier — teams with well-governed design systems get better AI code generation (via Figma MCP + Code Connect). Traditional metrics still apply: Grammarly 25% dev time saved, Freshworks 28% customer service cost reduction. New metric: AI code generation accuracy with vs without design system context.

title: ROI of Having a Design System — Netguru
insights: ROI quantification: development velocity +40% (fintech case), design consistency 70% fewer UI inconsistencies, accessibility compliance 30% faster with DS-enforced patterns. Governance ROI: federated contribution model increases adoption rate 2x vs centralized-only. Measurement framework: baseline velocity before DS → 6-month velocity after DS → delta = ROI.

title: Calculating the ROI of Your Design System — Zeroheight Blog
insights: Zeroheight's ROI calculator model: (time saved per component * number of components * team hourly rate) - DS team cost = annual savings. Adds: reduced QA time (consistent components catch fewer bugs), faster onboarding, reduced design debt. Key: measure time-to-ship a new feature using DS components vs without. Most teams see break-even at ~25 reusable components.
